reasoning: The Lean statement encodes "unique positive integers a, n with a^(n+1) - (a+1)^n = 2001" as unique existence of a pair p : ℕ × ℕ with p.1 > 0 ∧ p.2 > 0 and the equation. The positivity conditions correctly capture "positive integers" since in ℕ, > 0 means ≥ 1. Although ℕ uses truncated subtraction, this is harmless here: the target value 2001 is positive, so any valid solution must satisfy a^(n+1) ≥ (a+1)^n, where ℕ subtraction agrees with integer subtraction. Conversely, if a^(n+1) < (a+1)^n then ℕ subtraction yields 0 ≠ 2001, so no spurious solutions are introduced. The known unique solution (13, 2) satisfies 13^3 - 14^2 = 2001 with a positive integer difference. Therefore the Lean formulation is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
